perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge that the confidential information is a valuable, special and unique asset belonging to the disclosing party and accordingly, that it is of the utmost importance to the disclosing party tha t the confidential information not be used to advance the interest of any persons other than the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that the confidential information is a valuable, special and unique asset belonging to the disclosing party; however, the receiving party retains the right to use the confidential information to advance its own interests.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the text to allow the receiving party to use confidential information for their own interests directly contradicts the primary purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, which is to protect the disclosing party's confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 23 of the Copyright Act provides protections against unauthorized use or reproduction of copyrighted material, which could be included within the definition of 'confidential information.' By explicitly allowing the receiving party to use the confidential information for their own interests, the modified text undermines the protection afforded by copyright law, especially if that information is copyrightable. This creates a conflict between the contract and the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Chapter 3 - Circular 92 | U.S. Copyright Office Home Copyright Law of the United States (Title 17) Chapter 3 Chapter 3 1 : Duration of Copyright PDF Version (125 KB) Back to top 301. Preemption with respect to other laws 2 (a) On and after January 1, 1978, all legal or equitable rights that are equivalent to any of the exclusive rights within the general scope of copyright as specified by section 106 in works of authorship that are fixed in a tangible medium of expression and come within the subject matter of copyright as specified by sections 102 and 103, whether created before or after that date and whether published or unpublished, are governed exclusively by this title. Thereafter, no person is entitled to any such right or equivalent right in any such work under the common law or statutes of any State. (b) Nothing in this title annuls or limits any rights or remedies under the common law or statutes of any State with respect to— (1) subject matter that does not come within the subject matter of copyright as specified by sections 102 and 103, including works of authorship not fixed in any tangible medium of expression; or (2) any cause of action arising from undertakings commenced before January 1, 1978; (3) activities violating legal or equitable rights that are not equivalent to any of the exclusive rights within the general scope of copyright as specified by section 106; or (4) State and local landmarks, historic preservation, zoning, or building codes, relating to architectural works protected under section 102(a)(8). (c) Notwithstanding the provisions of section 303 , and in accordance with chapter 14, no sound recording fixed before February 15, 1972, shall be subject to copyright under this title. With respect to sound recordings fixed before February 15, 1972, the preemptive provisions of subsection (a) shall apply to activities that are commenced on and after the date of enactment of the Classics Protection and Access Act. Nothing in this subsection may be construed to affirm or negate the preemption of rights and remedies pertaining to any cause of action arising from the nonsubscription broadcast transmission of sound recordings under the common law or statutes of any State for activities that do not
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement (NDA) by allowing the receiving party to use confidential information for their own benefit. The original text strictly prohibits such use. The Law Explanation points to copyright law potentially being undermined if confidential information is copyrightable. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses copyright law and preemption, it's complex and not directly applicable without more context. However, the Perturbation Explanation clearly states that the modified text contradicts the primary purpose of an NDA. The modification fundamentally alters the agreement's core tenet of protecting the disclosing party's confidential information, thus creating a legal contradiction because it permits actions the original text and the very nature of NDAs are designed to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: BEE Online shall only provide information about its activities to SANAS or the DTI on request . The MD will acknowledge receipt of request by email within 2 days and will be responsible for the release of the information within 7 days. Before submitting a Measured Enterprise’s Confidential Information when requested by DTI, SANAS or required by Law, B EE Online shall notify the Measured Enterprise within 2 days of receiving the written request by email and will disclose the information within 7 days after Measured Enterprise has been notified BEE Online
perturbation.changed_text: BEE Online may provide information about its activities to SANAS or the DTI on request. The MD will acknowledge receipt of request by email within 10 days and will be responsible for the release of the information within 30 days. Before submitting a Measured Enterprise’s Confidential Information when requested by DTI, SANAS or required by Law, BEE Online may notify the Measured Enterprise when convenient by standard mail and will disclose the information when reasonably practical after Measured Enterprise has been notified BEE Online
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timelines and method of notification creates uncertainty about BEE Online's obligation to protect confidential information and inform the Measured Enterprise. Using terms like "when convenient" and "when reasonably practical" makes the disclosure process discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000 (Act No. 2 of 2000)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 23 of PAIA outlines grounds for refusal of access to records, including protection of commercial information of third parties. By making the notification and disclosure process discretionary, BEE Online could potentially violate PAIA by failing to adequately protect the confidential information of the Measured Enterprise and failing to adhere to the principles of justifiable and timely information release under the Act.
perturbation.justification: The original text establishes specific timelines and methods for notification and disclosure, ensuring a structured approach to handling confidential information. The changed text introduces discretionary language like "when convenient" and "when reasonably practical," creating uncertainty about BEE Online's obligations. While the provided snippet from PAIA doesn't offer explicit guidelines regarding timelines, the Law Explanation suggests that PAIA requires justifiable and timely information release. The modified text contradicts this principle by potentially delaying or neglecting the notification and disclosure process, hindering the Measured Enterprise's ability to protect its confidential information. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions (in this case, 'timely' information release), making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may request in writing at any time that any confidential information or any documents containing such confidential information, disclosed pursuant to the terms of this agreement and any copies thereof be returned with a written statement to the effect that upon such return the receiving party has not knowingly r etained in its possession or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such confidential information or any documents containing such confidential information or any copies thereof and the receiving party shall comply with any such request with in seven days of receipt of such request.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may request that any confidential information be returned with a statement that the receiving party believes it has not retained such information. The receiving party shall endeavor to comply with such request when feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the obligation to return confidential information and the required verification statement undermines the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Communications and Transactions Act 25 of 2002
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 82 of the ECT Act addresses the protection of personal information and data. While this NDA focuses on confidential information more broadly, the weakening of the return obligation means less control and assurance that electronically stored confidential information is properly handled or destroyed, potentially running afoul of data protection principles underlying the ECT Act.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates the return of confidential information and a written statement confirming no retention of such information within seven days. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the receiving party shall 'endeavor to comply' when 'feasible,' and only requires a statement that the party 'believes' it has not retained the information. The Perturbation Explanation states that weakening the obligation to return confidential information and the required verification statement undermines the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this could run afoul of data protection principles. Scraped Law Snippet 2, while seemingly generic, highlights the importance of respecting IP and laws, treaties, and judgements, suggesting the original stronger obligation aligns better with IP protection principles. The change introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection of confidential information, contradicting the need for stringent data control inferred from the general emphasis on IP protection.

perturbation.original_text: Should either party (the “defaulting party”) breach any provision of this agreement and fail to remedy such breach within seven days after receiving written notice from the other party (the “aggrieved party”) requiring it to do so, then the aggrieved party shall be entitled, without prejudice to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled at common law or in equity including inter alia injunction and specific performance. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary cont ained in this agreement, neither party shall be entitled to cancel this agreement in any circumstances whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: Should either party breach any provision of this agreement, the aggrieved party may seek mediation. Termination of this agreement is at the sole discretion of the breaching party.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the aggrieved party to mediation and giving the breaching party the sole right to terminate the agreement significantly weakens the remedies available for a breach of contract. It removes the aggrieved party's right to claim damages or seek an injunction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles under South African Common Law
perturbation.law_citation: South African Law of Contract - Remedies for Breach
perturbation.law_explanation: Under South African common law, remedies for breach of contract typically include damages, specific performance, and cancellation (depending on the severity of the breach). By removing the aggrieved party's right to claim damages or seek an injunction and giving the breaching party sole discretion over termination, the modified text contradicts fundamental principles of contract law regarding remedies for breach, making it significantly harder for the aggrieved party to enforce their rights. This creates an unconscionable imbalance of power. Note that there isn't one perfect URL for South African Common Law principles because it is based on precedent. The two URLs above help explain the current legal framework.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Comprender las dificultades de las obligaciones SPED para impuestos en Brasil - Withholding Tax - Brazil ARTICLE 29 March 2019 Comprender las dificultades de las obligaciones SPED para impuestos en Brasil TG TMF Group BV More Contributor TMF Group experts work from 120 offices in 80+ jurisdictions, making sure that complex administrative tasks are done right and on time. From legal set-up and oversight to regulatory filings, accounting, tax and payroll, we look after our clients’ administrative burdens so they can focus on their businesses. Explore Firm Details Las compañías brasileras tienen una elevada carga tributaria con obligaciones a cumplir complicadas y en constante cambio. Estas obligaciones son parte de un sistema digital especializado en Brasil llamado SPED. Brazil Tax Stacey Palker Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. Las compañías brasileras tienen una elevada carga tributaria con obligaciones a cumplir complicadas y en constante cambio. Estas obligaciones son parte de un sistema digital especializado en Brasil llamado SPED. Las compañías en Brasil deben cumplir con el SPED, que se traduce como Sistema Público de Escrituración Digital. Este sistema se creó para estandarizar y modernizar la administración tributaria en Brasil y reemplazar las copias de papel de las facturas y los registros fiscales. Si bien el sistema se implementó en 2008, el mismo está en constante evolución con nuevas obligaciones que surgen para los contribuyentes y las compañías, y que pueden resultar muy complejos. SPED en Brasil El sistema SPED es un universo tecnológico que recopila toda la información financiera, contable, fiscal y laboral de las compañías brasileras. SPED está compuesto por 12 módulos, con 7 obligaciones complementarias y 5 documentos fiscales diferentes. El hecho de contar con toda esta información en un solo lugar hace que sea más fácil para las autoridades fiscales advertir cualquier evasión fiscal o actividad criminal de las compañías. Los cinco documentos fiscales de SPED son facturas electrónicas que sirven para diferentes propósitos: NF-e: es un documento digital que valida la operación y los beneficios de las transacciones comerciales a través del uso de firmas electrónicas, tanto del emisor como de la administración fiscal. NFS-e: son facturas fiscales electrónicas para bienes y servicios. CT-e: se trata de un documento digital para la prestación de servicios de transporte de cargas que requiere también de las firmas digitales. NFC-e: hace referencia a la factura para consumidores que son, habitualmente, personas naturales y no compañías. MDF-e: se trata de un manifiesto fiscal electrónico. Las siete obligaciones complementarias son: eSocial : se trata de un programa para simplificar los RR.HH. y la nómina a fin de presentar toda la información del empleado a nivel mensual. ECD: corresponde a los documentos contables digitales que se envían una vez al año antes del 31 de mayo. ECF: es una devolución digital de impuestos que se creó en 2014 y que se envía una vez al año antes del 31 de julio. EFD - Contribuciones: se trata de una obligación mensual para presentar documentos fiscales en formato digital a las autoridades fiscales federales (PIS y Cofins). EFD - ICMS/IPI: hace referencia a una obligación mensual para presentar documentos fiscales en formato digital a las autoridades fiscales estatales (CISM e IPI). EFD-ReInf: es la contabilidad digital y la presentación de informes mensual de impuestos de retención e información sobre contribuciones de Seguridad Social. e-Financiera: es una obligación de cumplimiento para los bancos. La nueva obligación complementaria para las compañías en Brasil aumenta el alcance de validación de los impuestos de retención. Se denomina Escrituración Fiscal Digital de Retenciones y Otra Información Fiscal (EFD - REINF) y las compañías deben presentar la contabilidad de ganancias pagas y las retenciones del I
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text significantly weakens the remedies available to the aggrieved party in case of a breach, and grants termination rights solely to the breaching party. The Law Explanation states that under South African common law, remedies for breach of contract include damages, specific performance, and cancellation. The changed text contradicts this by restricting the aggrieved party to mediation and granting termination rights to the breaching party, thus removing the right to claim damages or seek an injunction. This creates an imbalance of power and undermines the fundamental principles of contract law.
